albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Digital Love, One More Time, and Crescendolls all sitting in your all-time top tracks, this is the album that generated more of your most-played moments than almost anything else in your library — time to live in it again.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time, and it lives here — this is the Radiohead album most drenched in the warm, textured sadness that clearly resonates deepest with you.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sit at nearly 1200 all-time plays for you, and this is the cornerstone — a record that rewards returning to after a long gap in a way few albums can match.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Röyksopp are your fifth most-played artist of all time and this debut is where they perfected the slow, luminous electronic atmosphere you clearly love — if it's been a while, it'll feel like coming home.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air have accumulated over 1000 plays in your library and this is their warmest, most transportive record — perfect comfort-food listening from the vault.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is your fourth most-played track of all time and it lives on this album — Leftism is the full vision, and you haven't sat with the whole thing in a while.

albums.title: Is This It
albums.reason: The Strokes sit at 652 all-time plays and this debut is where their energy was most concentrated and alive — a record that still sounds effortlessly cool every time.

albums.title: Dubnobasswithmyheadman
albums.reason: Born Slippy is in your all-time top 20 tracks and Underworld have 633 lifetime plays — this is the album where they built their whole world, and it's one of the great full listens in electronic music.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: You clearly love the textural, emotional side of electronic music — Daft Punk, Röyksopp, Air — and Boards of Canada's debut is the cult classic that slots perfectly into that lineage, full of warmth and strange beauty.

albums.title: Mezzanine
albums.reason: Given your love of Leftfield, Underworld, and the deeper end of electronic music, Mezzanine's brooding, layered atmospheres are exactly in your lane — this is a record that rewards obsessive listening.

albums.title: Dummy
albums.reason: Your deep affection for James Blake's melancholy and Radiohead's emotional weight points straight toward Portishead — Dummy is one of the most perfectly realised mood records of the 90s and likely a gap in your collection.

albums.title: Genetic World
albums.reason: If Air and Röyksopp's lush, romantic electronics speak to you, Télépopmusik's debut sits in the same French-adjacent world — polished, sensual, and criminally overlooked.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Your taste runs deep into atmospheric, textured electronica — Orbital, Leftfield, Justice — and Trentemøller's debut is the kind of late-night, emotionally resonant record that fits that world perfectly.

albums.title: The K&D Sessions
albums.reason: Given your love of deep electronic music and the more spacious, dubbed textures in your listening history, this legendary double album of remixes is essential — it's the missing link between your club-facing and chill-out tastes.

albums.title: Simple Things
albums.reason: The Air and Röyksopp plays tell me you love beautiful, melodic, slightly melancholic electronic music — Zero 7's debut has that same quality in abundance and belongs in your library.

albums.title: Supermodified
albums.reason: Your high play counts for Orbital and Justice suggest a real appetite for electronic music with structural ambition — Amon Tobin's Supermodified is a cult classic of chopped, sculpted sound that rewards deep listening.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: Your recent deep dives into Oscar Peterson, Bill Withers, and Charles Bradley suggest a strong pull toward soulful, rhythmically alive music — Yussef Kamaal's Black Focus is a modern London jazz classic that sits right at that intersection.

albums.title: Crush
albums.reason: Your recent arc — Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, DJ Koze, Four Tet — is all about music that blurs electronic texture with emotional depth, and Floating Points' Crush does exactly that with surgical precision.

albums.title: Con Todo El Mundo
albums.reason: The Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, and Sault plays show a clear love for soulful, groove-rooted music — Khruangbin's second album is the perfect bridge between that warmth and the more psychedelic end of your taste.

albums.title: Shadow Work
albums.reason: Your heavy recent listening to Hania Rani, Nils Frahm, and Brambles points toward music that is still, contemplative, and emotionally precise — Mammal Hands make modern jazz in exactly that spirit.

albums.title: Midnight in Peckham
albums.reason: Your recent plays of DJ Koze, Four Tet, and the various jazz-adjacent house tracks in your feed suggest an appetite for music in this groove — Chaos in the CBD's EP is a short, perfect late-night listen.

albums.title: Structuralism
albums.reason: The combination of your Oscar Peterson love and recent Bill Withers obsession suggests Alfa Mist's blend of jazz piano, hip-hop rhythm, and quiet soul would resonate deeply with where you are right now.

albums.title: v2.0
albums.reason: You've been living in Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and Martin Kohlstedt — contemplative piano-led music with electronic texture — and GoGo Penguin's v2.0 is the jazz-trio version of that impulse, urgent and beautiful.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: Your recent listens to Apparat, Max Cooper, and Dominik Eulberg point toward electronic music with genuine emotional architecture — Rival Consoles' Persona is exactly that, a deeply felt modular record.

albums.title: Prehension
albums.reason: Joep Beving is your most-played artist of the last 7 days by a wide margin and you've been deep in Liminal — Prehension is where his voice was most raw and searching, and it pairs perfectly with that mood.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: Bill Evans is third on your monthly chart and you've been in New Jazz Conceptions — Waltz for Debby is the warmer, more intimate companion piece, and one of the most beautiful piano trio recordings ever made.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: Tom Misch has been strong for you this month with Full Circle, and Geography is where his sound first fully bloomed — more textured and varied, it'll reward you if you haven't gone back recently.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: Nightmares on Wax have been in your recent rotation and you've been deep in the jazzy, late-night end of things — Smokers Delight is their warmest, most hypnotic record and a perfect fit for this week's mood.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: Nils Frahm has been consistent across your monthly and weekly charts, and with Joep Beving dominating this week, you're clearly in a solo piano headspace — Solo is Frahm at his most intimate and unhurried.

albums.title: Starfruit
albums.reason: Moonchild appeared in your recent albums with Voyager, and your current mood — jazzy, warm, soulful — maps perfectly to Starfruit, their most melodically generous and emotionally open record.

albums.title: Leaves
albums.reason: Martyn Heyne showed up in your recent listens with Electric Intervals, and with the current week pointing so clearly toward delicate, contemplative music, Leaves — his earlier, quieter record — is the natural companion.

albums.title: We Get Requests
albums.reason: Oscar Peterson dominated your last month with 36 plays and Night Train is already in your rotation — We Get Requests is the warmer, more melodic sibling, full of the same trio magic but with an even more lyrical touch.

songs.title: The Wilhelm Scream
songs.reason: Postpone is your most-played track of all time and CMYK is in your top 20 — The Wilhelm Scream is the James Blake moment that might hit even harder on a revisit, a devastatingly beautiful piece of work.

songs.title: Halcyon + On + On
songs.reason: Orbital have 777 all-time plays and this is one of their most emotionally overwhelming pieces — if you haven't let it wash over you recently, it's overdue.

songs.title: Remind Me
songs.reason: Röyksopp are your fifth most-played artist all time and this track from Melody A.M. is one of their most timeless moments — warm, melancholic, and built to last.

songs.title: All I Need
songs.reason: Reckoner is your most-played Radiohead track but All I Need from In Rainbows is just as devastating — a slow, pulsing piece of music that earns its climax completely.

songs.title: D.A.N.C.E.
songs.reason: Justice have 771 all-time plays and this is their most joyful, life-affirming moment — sometimes the vault holds something that just makes you feel good, and this is it.

songs.title: Let It Be
songs.reason: The Beatles sit at 831 all-time plays and this is one of those songs that means something different every time you return to it — comfort food from the deepest part of the vault.

songs.title: Kashmir
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin have 964 all-time plays and Kashmir is the track that most rewards returning to after a long absence — enormous and unhurried in a way that few songs manage.

songs.title: Citizen Erased
songs.reason: Muse have 712 lifetime plays and this is arguably their greatest single piece of work — sprawling, technically staggering, and the kind of track you forget how good it is until it starts.

songs.title: Kong
songs.reason: You liked Bonobo's Kiara which tells me the warm, rhythmically alive end of downtempo is very much in your world — Kong from Black Sands is a deeper, more hypnotic cut that shows another side of what he does.

songs.title: Lebanese Blonde
songs.reason: Your love of Air, Röyksopp, and the more cinematic side of electronic music points directly at Thievery Corporation — Lebanese Blonde is one of the great downtempo tracks, smoky and effortlessly cool.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: Given your deep history with Underworld, Leftfield, and the emotional weight of James Blake, Moderat's Bad Kingdom is the track that connects those worlds — stark, beautiful, and slightly haunted.

songs.title: Eyen
songs.reason: Your Orbital and Leftfield plays suggest a real love for UK electronic music that has structure and warmth — Plaid are the criminally underrated peers of that scene and Eyen is their most accessible and affecting track.

songs.title: Gorecki
songs.reason: The emotional weight in your all-time listening — James Blake's fragility, Radiohead's melancholy — points toward Gorecki, one of the most heart-stopping tracks of the 90s downtempo era.

songs.title: Glue
songs.reason: Your love of Orbital, Justice, and the more euphoric end of electronic music suggests Bicep's Glue would hit hard — it's built from the same love of classic rave and house, just rebuilt for now.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: Daft Punk, Leftfield, Orbital — you clearly love electronic music that builds toward something overwhelming, and Open Eye Signal is one of the decade's best examples of exactly that.

songs.title: Space Is Only Noise If You Can See
songs.reason: The atmospheric, textural quality you love in Air and the deeper Daft Punk cuts points toward Nicolas Jaar — this opening track from his debut is slow, strange, and deeply beautiful.

songs.title: Ceremonial
songs.reason: Your recent arc — Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, Martin Kohlstedt — is all about music with space and emotional texture, and Anchorsong builds something similar from global percussion and electronic layering.

songs.title: Oneness
songs.reason: Your recent jazz listening — Oscar Peterson, Bill Withers' soul DNA, Charles Bradley — suggests a real love for music with depth and heart, and Matthew Halsall's Oneness is a meditation in exactly that spirit.

songs.title: Hyena
songs.reason: The Hania Rani and Nils Frahm plays suggest you love music that sits between classical precision and electronic feeling — Jaga Jazzist do something similar but with jazz instrumentation, and Hyena is one of their most thrilling pieces.

songs.title: Landslide
songs.reason: The Bonobo like, combined with your recent Four Tet and DJ Koze plays, points strongly toward Catching Flies — Landslide is a beautiful, unhurried piece that sits right in that warm electronic-organic space.

songs.title: Lucid
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Max Cooper and Dominik Eulberg shows an appetite for electronic music with real atmosphere — Kelly Lee Owens builds something dreamier and more sensual from similar materials.

songs.title: Gold
songs.reason: Your recent combination of Bill Withers soul, Four Tet electronica, and DJ Koze's warm house suggests Chet Faker's Gold would be a natural fit — it bridges all of those worlds in one track.

songs.title: Khmer
songs.reason: Your recent fascination with music that sits between jazz and electronic sound — Oscar Peterson alongside DJ Koze and Four Tet — points toward Nils Petter Molvær, who fused them in a way nobody else has.

songs.title: Butterscotch Poem
songs.reason: Your recent jazz and soul listening combined with the rhythmic electronic music in your feed — DJ Koze, Four Tet — suggests Makaya McCraven's looped, layered jazz would feel completely natural and alive to you.

songs.title: Sleeping Lotus
songs.reason: Joep Beving is dominating your last 7 days and you've been deep in Prehension-era material — Sleeping Lotus is one of his most quietly devastating individual pieces and a perfect fit for this week's headspace.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Bill Evans is third on your monthly chart and with the week pointing so strongly toward slow, contemplative piano music, Peace Piece — a single sustained meditation — is exactly the track this moment calls for.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Nils Frahm has been present all month and this week, and Says is the track that shows the other side of his quieter work — slowly building from stillness into something overwhelming and beautiful.

songs.title: Nightrider
songs.reason: Tom Misch has been strong this month and with scruscru's jazzy house also in heavy rotation, Nightrider sits right at the intersection — a late-night, softly grooving track with real emotional pull.

songs.title: The List
songs.reason: Moonchild appeared in your recent albums and the current mood is clearly warm and introspective — The List is one of their most affecting songs, unhurried and emotionally generous.

songs.title: You Wish
songs.reason: Nightmares on Wax have been in your recent rotation and you've been in a jazzy, late-night groove all week — You Wish is one of their warmest and most soulful moments, a perfect right-now track.

songs.title: South Wind, Clear Sky
songs.reason: scruscru are your second most-played artist of the last 7 days and South Wind, Clear Sky is the title track of one of their most complete statements — a natural next step from what you've already been playing this week.

songs.title: A Moi La Liberté
songs.reason: Folamour showed up in your recent tracks this week and the current mood — jazzy, soulful, warm — is exactly where this track lives, a piece of house music that feels like a genuine emotional release.

songs.title: Nacht
songs.reason: Felix Rösch has been in both your recent albums and current track plays, and with the week so clearly defined by quiet piano music, Nacht captures exactly the still, nocturnal quality that's running through everything you're playing right now.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
